methodology: Cross-referenced FatSecret generic entries, CalorieKing restaurant data, USDA ingredient-level calculations, and Nutritionix averages. Italian restaurants rarely publish nutrition facts — values are ESTIMATES based on typical recipes and standard restaurant portions. Restaurant portions are significantly larger than homemade (typically 2-3 cups pasta per plate, liberal butter/oil/cream). Calorie ranges reflect variation across restaurants.

items.notes: THE worst offender on an Italian menu. Heavy cream + butter + parmesan on 2.5-3 cups pasta. FatSecret generic ~520 cal/cup but restaurants serve 2.5+ cups with extra cream. CalorieKing restaurant averages 1200-1500 cal per plate. Some chains hit 1800+. The cream sauce alone is 400-500 cal.
items.lean_version: Ask for grilled chicken + garlic & olive oil sauce instead of alfredo. Or half portion with a side salad. Saves 500-700 cal.

items.notes: Tomato-based meat sauce is significantly leaner than cream sauces. USDA ground beef + tomato sauce ~180 cal/cup sauce. Main calorie driver is the 2.5 cups of pasta underneath (~550 cal). Restaurants often add olive oil to finish. Better than Alfredo but still calorie-dense from pasta volume.
items.lean_version: Ask for half pasta, double meat sauce. Or sub zucchini noodles if available. Saves 200-300 cal.

items.notes: Breaded chicken cutlet (fried) + mozzarella + marinara + side of spaghetti. The breading + frying adds 300-400 cal to otherwise lean chicken. FatSecret generic chicken parm ~340 cal per piece without pasta. Add 550 cal for the spaghetti side. Cheese adds another 150-200. Restaurant portions are massive.
items.lean_version: Order chicken parm WITHOUT the pasta side. Ask for steamed broccoli or side salad instead. Saves 400-550 cal. Even better: ask for grilled (not breaded) chicken with marinara and light cheese.

items.notes: Tomato cream sauce (vodka + heavy cream + tomato + parmesan). Feels lighter than Alfredo because it's pink but still has 1/2 cup+ cream per serving. The vodka cooks off but the cream doesn't. Sneaky calorie bomb because people think 'tomato sauce = lighter'. It's not.
items.lean_version: Swap to marinara sauce (saves 300+ cal from eliminated cream). Or ask for 'vodka sauce on the side' and use half.

items.notes: FatSecret generic lasagna with meat: 336 cal for a small piece (1/6 of 8" square). Restaurant pieces are 50-75% bigger. Layers of pasta, ricotta, mozzarella, meat sauce, parmesan. Cheese content is the main calorie driver. Some restaurants bake with extra butter/oil on top.
items.lean_version: Not much you can do to modify this one. Portion control is the play — eat half, box the rest. Or skip the bread basket to offset.

items.notes: Egg yolks + guanciale/pancetta + pecorino + black pepper on pasta. Authentic carbonara uses no cream, but American-Italian restaurants often ADD cream, making it even heavier. The egg-cheese emulsion creates a rich sauce. Guanciale/pancetta adds 200+ cal of rendered pork fat. Comparable to Alfredo in calorie density.
items.lean_version: Ask for a lunch portion or half order. There's no light version of carbonara — it IS the fat. Pair with a side salad instead of bread.

items.notes: Pecorino Romano + black pepper + pasta water emulsion. Sounds simple but uses 1-1.5 cups grated pecorino per plate (pecorino = 110 cal/oz). Restaurant versions often add butter to make the sauce smoother. Fewer ingredients doesn't mean fewer calories — the cheese IS the sauce.
items.lean_version: Ask for a half portion. Or order as a side/appetizer portion instead of entree. The only lever is volume — you can't remove the cheese.

items.notes: The 'healthy choice' that isn't as healthy as people think. Mixed vegetables (zucchini, bell pepper, broccoli, tomato) in olive oil or light cream sauce over pasta. Vegetables add 50-80 cal, but the 2+ cups of pasta + 3-4 tbsp olive oil bring it to 700+. Some restaurants use cream, pushing it to 850+.
items.lean_version: Ask for 'extra vegetables, less pasta' or sub zucchini noodles. Request olive oil on the side. Can get it down to 400-500 cal.

items.notes: Shrimp in garlic butter + white wine over linguine. FatSecret: shrimp scampi (no pasta) = 288 cal/cup (68% fat from butter). Add 550 cal for the linguine underneath. The butter-wine sauce sounds light but restaurants use 4-6 tbsp butter per plate. Shrimp itself is lean (7 cal each) but drowns in butter.
items.lean_version: Order shrimp scampi WITHOUT linguine — get it over steamed vegetables or side salad. Cuts to ~350-400 cal. Ask for 'light butter' or 'olive oil instead of butter'.

items.notes: Ziti pasta baked with ricotta, mozzarella, marinara, sometimes Italian sausage. Similar to lasagna but less structured. Cheese melts into the pasta making every bite calorie-dense. If made with sausage, add 150-200 cal. The baked cheese crust on top is another 100-150 cal.
items.lean_version: Ask if they have a version without ricotta, just marinara and light mozzarella. Or eat half. Similar strategy to lasagna.

items.notes: FatSecret: 582 cal/cup but that includes extra sauce. Chicken breast sauteed in butter, marsala wine, mushrooms. The wine sauce isn't as heavy as cream but restaurants use 3-4 tbsp butter to finish. Mushrooms add negligible calories. Decent protein-to-calorie ratio for Italian. The pan sauce is the calorie variable.
items.lean_version: One of the better Italian picks. Ask for sauce on the side and use sparingly. Pair with vegetables instead of pasta. Gets to ~350 cal.

items.notes: FatSecret: 389 cal/cup. Chicken breast sauteed with lemon, capers, white wine, butter. THE lean Italian order. Lemon-caper sauce is the lightest classic Italian sauce. Capers add flavor for zero calories. Some restaurants flour-dredge the chicken (+50 cal). Still one of the best protein-to-calorie ratios on any Italian menu.
items.lean_version: Already the lean pick. Ask for extra lemon, light butter. Pair with steamed broccoli or side salad. Total meal: 450-500 cal with incredible protein.

items.notes: Same preparation as chicken piccata but with veal cutlet. Veal is slightly higher in fat than chicken breast (~170 cal/4oz vs 130 cal/4oz). Lemon-caper-wine sauce keeps it relatively lean. Still a strong pick compared to pasta dishes. USDA veal cutlet ~180 cal/4oz.
items.lean_version: Same as chicken piccata — ask for light butter, pair with vegetables. The piccata preparation is inherently one of the lightest.

items.notes: USDA Atlantic salmon ~208 cal/4oz raw. 8oz restaurant fillet grilled with olive oil. Fat is mostly healthy omega-3s. Restaurants often add herb butter on top (+100 cal). Without added butter, one of the best macro profiles on any Italian menu. High protein, zero carbs, healthy fats.
items.lean_version: Ask for 'no butter on top, just lemon.' Pair with grilled vegetables. Meal total: 500-550 cal with 50g+ protein. Elite Italian order.

items.notes: European sea bass, typically grilled whole with olive oil and lemon. One of the leanest fish options. ~130 cal/4oz raw. Restaurant adds olive oil during grilling (+120 cal). Exceptionally high protein-to-calorie ratio. Often served with lemon and herbs. The bones make you eat slower = natural portion control.
items.lean_version: Already one of the leanest things on any Italian menu. Ask for light oil, extra lemon. With a side salad: ~450 cal total meal with 52g protein.

items.notes: Thin-pounded chicken breast, lightly floured, sauteed with white wine and lemon or mushroom sauce. Very similar to piccata. The thin pounding means more surface area = more butter absorption, but portions are controlled. Restaurants vary on sauce richness. A solid lean pick.
items.lean_version: Ask for lemon-wine sauce (lightest option). Pair with steamed vegetables. Gets to ~350-400 cal.

items.notes: San Marzano tomato, fresh mozzarella, basil, olive oil on regular crust. The 'simplest' pizza is still 270 cal/slice from dough + cheese + oil. Fresh mozzarella has less fat than low-moisture but restaurants use generous amounts. 2 slices = 540 cal. 3 slices = 810 cal. Most people eat 3-4 slices.
items.lean_version: Ask for light cheese or well-done (less moisture = feels more substantial). Stop at 2 slices with a side salad.

items.notes: Regular crust with mozzarella and pepperoni. Each pepperoni slice ~5 cal, ~15-20 per slice of pizza. Grease pools from rendered pepperoni fat add ~30-40 cal vs plain cheese. 3 slices = 930 cal. Blotting the grease with a napkin actually saves ~30-40 cal per slice (not a joke).
items.lean_version: Blot the grease (seriously, saves 30-40 cal/slice). Limit to 2 slices + salad. Or ask for light cheese.

items.notes: Pepperoni, sausage, bacon, ham, sometimes ground beef on regular crust. The highest calorie pizza per slice. All the rendered meat fat soaks into the dough. 3 slices = 1,140 cal. The multiple meat layers each contribute 30-50 extra cal. The crust absorbs the grease from above.
items.lean_version: If you want meat on pizza, just get pepperoni (one protein, not four). Or chicken + veggie. Saves 80-100 cal/slice vs meat lovers.

items.notes: Bell peppers, onions, mushrooms, olives, tomatoes on cheese pizza. Lowest calorie pizza option per slice. Vegetables add volume and fiber with minimal calories. Olives add some fat (~5 cal each). Still mostly dough and cheese calories. The 'healthiest' pizza is still pizza.
items.lean_version: The best pizza pick if you must have pizza. Ask for light cheese, extra veggies, well-done. 2 slices + salad = reasonable 580-cal meal.

items.product_name: White Pizza / Ricotta Pizza (per slice, 14" large)
items.notes: No tomato sauce — ricotta, mozzarella, garlic, olive oil on crust. Replacing tomato sauce with ricotta + extra olive oil adds 50-70 cal per slice vs margherita. Looks lighter because it's white but is actually one of the heavier options. Double cheese (ricotta + mozzarella) = double the dairy fat.
items.lean_version: Ask for light ricotta. Or just get margherita instead — the tomato sauce has fewer calories than the ricotta layer.

items.product_name: Thin Crust Pizza - Margherita (per slice, 14" large)
items.notes: Thin crust saves ~70 cal per slice vs regular crust. The crust is the biggest calorie component of any pizza (dough = flour + oil + sugar). Less dough = less carbs + less fat. Same toppings, just less bread underneath. 3 thin crust slices ≈ 2 regular crust slices calorie-wise.
items.lean_version: This IS the lean version. Thin crust + veggie or margherita + salad = the best Italian pizza meal you can build.

items.notes: Toasted bread rubbed with garlic, topped with diced tomatoes, basil, olive oil. Seems healthy — it's mostly bread + olive oil. Most orders come as 4-6 pieces = 480-720 cal for an 'appetizer.' The bread is often brushed with olive oil before toasting (+40-60 cal per piece). Tomato topping is only ~15 cal.
items.lean_version: Limit to 1-2 pieces. The tomato topping is fine — it's the oiled bread underneath that adds up. Or skip and get a caprese salad instead.

items.notes: Squid rings battered and deep-fried, served with marinara. Squid itself is very lean (~100 cal/4oz) but the flour batter + deep frying triples the calories. Marinara dip is fine (~15 cal/tbsp) but some restaurants serve with aioli (+90 cal/tbsp). A shared appetizer is fine; eating the whole order solo is a 540-cal appetizer before your entree.
items.lean_version: Split with the table. Choose marinara over aioli dip. Or ask if they have grilled calamari (half the calories, same protein).

items.notes: Fresh mozzarella, tomato slices, basil, olive oil, balsamic. Sounds healthy and IS relatively healthy — but fresh mozzarella is 280 cal/cup and restaurants use 4-6oz. The olive oil drizzle adds 120-240 cal depending on generosity. Most of the calories are healthy fats. Low carb. Decent protein from cheese.
items.lean_version: Ask for light olive oil or balsamic only. Still a solid appetizer choice — high protein, low carb, no fried anything.

items.notes: Cured meats (prosciutto, salami, sopressata), cheeses (provolone, mozzarella), olives, roasted peppers, marinated artichokes. Every component is calorie-dense: salami = 110 cal/oz, prosciutto = 60 cal/oz, provolone = 100 cal/oz. A full platter for the table can be 1600-2000 cal total. Even 1/4 of it is a full appetizer.
items.lean_version: Focus on the prosciutto (leanest cured meat), roasted peppers, and artichokes. Skip the salami and heavy cheeses. Or just get a caprese instead.

items.notes: Breaded and deep-fried mozzarella sticks with marinara. Each stick ~90 cal. The breading + frying adds ~40 cal per stick beyond the cheese itself. Similar calorie profile to fried calamari but less protein (cheese vs squid). Usually served as 6 pieces. A classic calorie trap disguised as a 'shared' appetizer.
items.lean_version: Eat 2, let the table have the rest. Or order caprese instead — same cheese, no breading, no frying.

items.notes: Italian bread brushed with garlic butter, sometimes topped with parmesan. The butter is the calorie bomb — 2 tbsp per slice is standard. With cheese, add 40-60 cal per slice. Most orders come as 4-6 slices. A seemingly innocent basket of garlic bread = 720-1080 cal. It arrives before your meal even does.
items.lean_version: Limit to 1 slice. Or skip entirely and save those 180 cal for your actual entree.

items.notes: Vegetable soup with beans, pasta, tomato broth. THE best Italian appetizer for weight loss. High fiber, high volume, low calorie. Fills you up before the entree arrives so you eat less pasta. USDA minestrone ~82 cal/cup but restaurant versions add olive oil and sometimes pasta pieces, bumping it up. Still the smartest starter on any Italian menu.
items.lean_version: Already the lean pick. A cup of minestrone before your entree = eat less of everything else. This is the hack.

items.notes: Romaine, parmesan, croutons, Caesar dressing. Caesar dressing is 80-90 cal per tbsp and restaurants use 3-4 tbsp. Croutons add 50-80 cal. Parmesan shavings add 40-60 cal. The romaine itself is ~10 cal. A 'salad' that's 85% fat calories. As an entree with chicken, add 200 cal. People order this thinking they're being healthy.
items.lean_version: Ask for dressing on the side, use half. Skip croutons. Light parmesan. Gets it down to 180-220 cal. Add grilled chicken for protein.

items.notes: Mixed greens, tomato, red onion, olives, pepperoncini, Italian vinaigrette. Lighter than Caesar because vinaigrette is ~45 cal/tbsp vs 80-90 for Caesar. Olives add 5 cal each. A reasonable starter if you control the dressing. Most restaurants pour 3-4 tbsp on, which is where the calories hide.
items.lean_version: Dressing on the side, always. Dip your fork in dressing, then spear the lettuce. You'll use 1/3 the dressing. Gets it to ~130 cal.

items.notes: Chopped romaine, salami, provolone, chickpeas, pepperoncini, olives, red onion, Italian dressing. The meats and cheese make this more like an antipasto salad than a 'healthy' salad. Salami = 110 cal/oz, provolone = 100 cal/oz. These add 200+ cal that a basic house salad doesn't have. Still better than pasta.
items.lean_version: Ask them to skip the salami, light cheese, dressing on the side. Gets it down to ~280 cal. Add grilled chicken for clean protein.

items.product_name: Italian Bread (from bread basket, per slice)
items.notes: Plain Italian bread from the basket. Seems innocent at 110 cal but nobody eats just one slice. The average person eats 3-4 slices while waiting for food = 330-440 cal BEFORE the meal. With butter or olive oil dip, it's 200+ cal per slice. The bread basket is the silent calorie killer of Italian dining.
items.lean_version: Ask them not to bring the bread basket. Seriously. If it's not on the table, you won't eat it. This single move saves 300-500 cal per meal.

items.product_name: Bread with Olive Oil Dip (per tbsp oil)
items.notes: USDA: olive oil = 119 cal per tablespoon. Restaurants put out 3-5 tbsp in the dipping plate = 360-600 cal of pure fat sitting there. Combined with bread: 3 slices bread + 3 tbsp oil = 690 cal. That's an entire meal's worth of calories consumed unconsciously while 'waiting for food.' The bread basket + oil is the biggest hidden calorie trap in Italian dining.
items.lean_version: If you must dip, use balsamic vinegar instead of olive oil (14 cal/tbsp vs 120). Or just skip the bread entirely.

items.notes: Italian flatbread baked with olive oil, salt, sometimes rosemary. Higher in fat than regular Italian bread because olive oil is mixed into and brushed on top of the dough. Each piece has 1-2 tbsp oil baked in. Restaurants often serve this warm, which makes it irresistible. 2 pieces = 420 cal before your meal.
items.lean_version: Limit to 1 piece and skip the olive oil dip. Or ask the server to not bring bread at all.

items.notes: FatSecret: 283 cal per 100g. Restaurant slices are typically 140-170g. Ladyfingers + mascarpone + espresso + cocoa + sugar. Mascarpone is 120 cal/oz (fattier than cream cheese). The ladyfingers soak up the coffee-mascarpone mixture like sponges. Some restaurants add rum or Kahlua which adds sugar calories.
items.lean_version: Split with someone. No lean version exists — it IS mascarpone and sugar. Half a serving = 210 cal, which is a reasonable dessert.

items.notes: Fried pastry shell filled with sweetened ricotta + chocolate chips. The shell is deep-fried dough (like a small churro). The filling is ricotta + powdered sugar + sometimes mascarpone. Mini cannoli (~50g) = 185 cal if your restaurant offers them. Some places dip the ends in chocolate or pistachios, adding 30-50 cal.
items.lean_version: Ask for a mini cannoli if available (half the calories). Or share one. No way to make a cannoli lean — it's fried dough and sweetened cheese.

items.notes: FatSecret: 223 cal per 100g. Cooked cream + sugar + gelatin, set like custard. Restaurant portions ~120-140g. The lightest Italian dessert option by volume — it's mostly cream set with gelatin. But the sugar content is high (22g/100g). Berry coulis topping adds 20-30 cal. Among Italian desserts, this is actually the most reasonable.
items.lean_version: The best Italian dessert option calorie-wise. Ask for berry sauce instead of caramel. Or split one.

items.notes: USDA: basic marinara ~35 cal per 1/4 cup. Tomato, garlic, basil, olive oil. The lightest sauce on any Italian menu. Restaurants add more olive oil than homemade, but it's still far leaner than cream-based sauces. Always choose marinara over alfredo, vodka, or butter sauces.
items.lean_version: Already the lean pick for sauces. Use this as your default dip and pasta sauce.

items.notes: Heavy cream + butter + parmesan cheese, reduced. This is the sauce equivalent of drinking melted butter. Per 1/2 cup: ~3 tbsp butter + 1/2 cup heavy cream + 1/2 cup parmesan. Cream = 400 cal/cup, butter = 100 cal/tbsp. On a plate of pasta, the sauce alone is 400-500 cal. The difference between marinara and alfredo is literally 375 calories of pure fat.
items.lean_version: Swap to marinara. Period. Saves 375 cal instantly. If you want creamy, ask for a light garlic olive oil sauce instead.

items.notes: Tomato + heavy cream + vodka + parmesan. The 'middle ground' sauce — lighter than alfredo, heavier than marinara. The cream is what separates it from marinara: ~1/4 cup cream per serving = 200 cal. Vodka cooks off (no alcohol calories) but the cream stays. People think the pink color means 'light.' It doesn't.
items.lean_version: Ask for 'marinara with a splash of cream' instead of full vodka sauce. Or just go full marinara. Saves 195+ cal.

items.notes: Basil, pine nuts, parmesan, garlic, olive oil blended. Calorie-dense because it's mostly olive oil and pine nuts. 1/4 cup is standard on pasta but restaurants are generous. At least the fats are from olive oil and nuts (unsaturated). Better fat profile than alfredo but similar calorie density. Pine nuts = 190 cal/oz.
items.lean_version: Ask for 'light pesto' or pesto on the side. A little goes a long way flavor-wise. Using half the pesto saves 130 cal.

items.notes: Chicken marsala (480 cal) served with typical restaurant pasta side (~1.5 cups spaghetti or mashed potatoes, ~350 cal). The entree itself is reasonable but the mandatory pasta/starch side doubles the calories. This is why you always need to specify 'no pasta side' or 'sub vegetables.'
items.lean_version: Ask for steamed broccoli or sauteed spinach instead of pasta side. Drops to ~530-550 cal with excellent protein.

items.notes: Chicken piccata (390 cal) with pasta side (~350 cal). Even the leanest entree gets heavy with a pasta side. This is why Italian restaurants are calorie traps — every entree comes with 300-400 cal of starch you didn't ask for. Specify your side or you get pasta by default.
items.lean_version: Sub vegetables for pasta. Chicken piccata + steamed veggies + side salad = 480-520 cal total. Best complete Italian meal.

items.notes: Grilled salmon (460 cal) with pasta or risotto side (~350 cal). Great protein-to-calorie ratio if you ditch the starch side. Salmon + pasta is still better than most pasta entrees because at least you're getting 50g+ protein.
items.lean_version: Sub roasted vegetables or side salad for pasta. Total: ~520 cal with 54g protein and healthy omega-3 fats.

items.notes: Breaded and fried eggplant slices layered with marinara and mozzarella. The 'vegetarian option' that isn't lean. Eggplant absorbs oil like a sponge during frying — up to 2 tbsp oil per slice. Then covered in cheese. Without pasta side. With pasta, add 350 cal. People order this thinking 'vegetable = healthy.'
items.lean_version: Ask if they can grill the eggplant instead of frying. Or order grilled vegetables with marinara and light mozzarella. Saves 200+ cal.

items.notes: Spaghetti with garlic, olive oil, red pepper flakes. The 'simplest' pasta dish. No cream, no cheese, no meat — just pasta + oil. Still 750 cal because restaurants use 4-6 tbsp olive oil (480-720 cal of oil alone) on 2+ cups pasta. Proves that simple doesn't mean low-calorie. The oil IS the sauce.
items.lean_version: Ask for light oil. Or get this as a side portion and pair with a protein entree. Half portion = 375 cal.

items.notes: Arborio rice cooked with broth, butter, parmesan, wine. Restaurants finish with 3-4 tbsp butter + heavy parmesan. The constant stirring creates a creamy texture from starch, but restaurants add butter for extra richness. Rice absorbs the broth (low cal) but also absorbs the butter (high cal). With added mushrooms ~15 cal; with added shrimp ~100 cal.
items.lean_version: Ask for light butter finish. Or get risotto as a side (half portion). Seafood risotto is the best option — adds protein.

items.notes: Braised veal shank in white wine, broth, tomatoes. The slow braising renders some fat out. Good protein-to-calorie ratio for a rich dish. Usually served with gremolata (parsley, garlic, lemon zest — negligible calories) over risotto or polenta. Without the risotto side, this is actually a decent macro pick.
items.lean_version: Skip the risotto side, ask for vegetables. The osso buco itself has excellent protein. Total with veggies: ~600 cal, 48g protein.

items.notes: Breaded and pan-fried veal cutlet. Like a big schnitzel. The breadcrumb coating + pan-frying in butter adds 200+ cal to the lean veal underneath. Often served with arugula salad on top (good, adds only 5 cal). The cutlet is pounded thin so more surface area = more breading = more oil absorption.
items.lean_version: Ask for grilled veal instead of breaded. Saves 150-200 cal. The arugula salad on top is great — keep that.

items.notes: Chicken broth with mini meatballs, escarole/spinach, small pasta. Slightly more caloric than minestrone due to meatballs but still an excellent starter. The meatballs add protein. Broth-based soups are always smart Italian starters — they fill you up before the calorie-heavy entrees arrive.
items.lean_version: Already a smart pick. Between this and minestrone, you can't go wrong. Both fill you up for under 160 cal.

biggest_traps: Bread basket + olive oil dip = 500-700 cal before the meal (3 bread slices + 3 tbsp oil = 690 cal)
Fettuccine Alfredo = 1,200-1,500 cal per restaurant plate (the worst single dish)
Cream sauces (Alfredo, Vodka, Carbonara) add 300-500 cal vs marinara
'Healthy' options like Pasta Primavera still hit 700+ cal from pasta + oil
Caesar salad = 360 cal mostly from dressing (more fat than protein)
Every protein entree comes with a 350-cal pasta side by default

lean_italian_order.starter: Minestrone soup (130 cal) OR cup of Italian wedding soup (150 cal)
lean_italian_order.entree: Chicken Piccata, Grilled Salmon, or Branzino (350-460 cal)
lean_italian_order.side: Steamed vegetables or side salad with dressing on the side (50-100 cal)
lean_italian_order.avoid: Ask them not to bring the bread basket. No cream sauces. No pasta side.

sauce_hierarchy_by_calories: Marinara = 65 cal per 1/2 cup (THE lean choice)
Pesto = 260 cal per 1/4 cup (healthy fats but calorie-dense)
Vodka = 260 cal per 1/2 cup (the sneaky one — pink doesn't mean light)
Alfredo = 440 cal per 1/2 cup (liquid butter, avoid)
pizza_rules: Thin crust saves ~70 cal per slice vs regular crust
Veggie or Margherita = lowest calorie per slice (200-270)
Meat Lovers = highest calorie per slice (380)
2 slices + salad = reasonable meal. 3+ slices = calorie bomb
Blotting grease with a napkin saves 30-40 cal per slice (real data)
